<!-- Topic 3: Index Notation -->
<!-- Slides 28-38 -->

# Index Notation
<!-- Slide 28 -->

## A Position Has a Number {.smaller}

+ How does a program name one specific value in a vector?
+ Each vector position has an index.

::: notes
Slides 28-38
:::

<!-- Slide 29 -->

---

## Zero-Based Indexing

Vector indexes start at `0`.

| Index | 0 | 1 | 2 |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Value | 88 | 92 | 75 |

The first value is at index `0`, not index `1`.

<!-- Slide 30 -->

---

## Index Picture

Think of the index as the address of one cell in the vector.

```cpp
vector<int> scores = {88, 92, 75};
```

| Expression | Value |
|---|---:|
| `scores[0]` | 88 |
| `scores[1]` | 92 |
| `scores[2]` | 75 |

<!-- Slide 31 -->

---

## Reading One Known Position

Use brackets when the program needs one known position.

```cpp
vector<int> scores = {88, 92, 75};

cout << scores[0] << endl;
```

This prints the first stored score.

<!-- Slide 32 -->

---

## Changing One Known Position

Index notation can also replace one existing value.

```cpp
vector<int> scores = {88, 92, 75};

scores[1] = 100;
```

The value at index `1` changes from `92` to `100`.

<!-- Slide 33 -->

---

## Execution Trace: Change One Element

| Statement | scores |
|---|---|
| `vector<int> scores = {88, 92, 75};` | `{88, 92, 75}` |
| `scores[1] = 100;` | `{88, 100, 75}` |

Only one position changes.

<!-- Slide 34 -->

---

## The Limit of Direct Access

Printing several positions by hand gets repetitive.

```cpp
cout << scores[0] << endl;
cout << scores[1] << endl;
cout << scores[2] << endl;
```

This works only when we know exactly how many values there are.

<!-- Slide 35 -->

---

## When We Want Every Value

Direct access is useful for one known position.

When the program needs every value, a loop is the better tool.

The next section introduces the simplest loop for vectors.

<!-- Slide 36 -->

---

## Common Practices

+ Use index notation when a specific position matters.
+ Remember that the first position is `0`.
+ Avoid guessing indexes when the vector size may change.

<!-- Slide 37 -->

---

## Summary

+ Index notation names one position in a vector.
+ It can read or replace a known value.
+ Loops are better when the whole vector matters.

<!-- Slide 38 -->
